network.overall_strategy: This network models glycine as a convergence product of ten literature-grounded prebiotic routes spanning hydrothermal, surface, and biochemical settings. The design is organized around experimentally supported hub molecules and feedstocks identified in the literature: HCN, formaldehyde, aminoacetonitrile, glyoxylate, and pyruvate. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced carbon intermediates (formate, formaldehyde, pyruvate) and a direct glyoxylate-to-glycine route on ferroan brucite; surface chemistry supplies HCN, nitriles, hydantoin intermediates, and warm-pond/ice-eutectic Strecker and HCN-polymer routes; biochemical/prebiotic settings perform hydrolysis, transamination-like completion, and peptide assembly. The ten target pathways correspond to: direct Strecker via aminoacetonitrile, Strecker via glycinamide, Bucherer-Bergs via hydantoin, Bucherer-Bergs via N-carbamoylglycine, HCN polymer hydrolysis, cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route, hydrothermal CO2-to-formaldehyde-enabled Strecker, hydrothermal pyruvate-to-glyoxylate-connected route, ferroan brucite glyoxylate+nitrate route, and FTT-derived formaldehyde/HCN-fed Strecker. All hydrothermal and surface reactions explicitly include mineral agents required by the specification; where the literature uses catalysts outside the required mineral lists, the literature catalyst is stated in conditions/reasoning while required mineral agents are included as geochemically associated mineral surfaces.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate and formaldehyde precursors

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H2; formaldehyde included as downstream C1 product consistent with hydrothermal carbon fixation network

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface hydrogenation of CO2 generates formate as major product with further reduced C1 carbon feeding methanol/formaldehyde-equivalent pools on vent mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) showed greigite and magnetite catalyze CO2 + H2 to formate under alkaline hydrothermal conditions at 100°C; Beyazay et al. (2023) demonstrated Ni3Fe-mediated CO2 fixation at 25°C/25 bar in serpentinizing systems. Formaldehyde is not the dominant reported product in Preiner et al., but inclusion as a connected reduced C1 feedstock is a conservative network bridge consistent with vent-derived reduced carbon and the review’s identification of CO2-to-C1 production in alkaline vents.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation to pyruvate

conditions.catalyst: greigite/magnetite with awaruite-like transition metal activity

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation couples reduced C1/C2 species to produce the alpha-keto acid pyruvate on iron-rich mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Preiner et al. (2020) detected pyruvate from CO2 + H2 on greigite/magnetite/awaruite at 100°C in alkaline hydrothermal conditions, and Beyazay et al. (2023) extended pyruvate formation to room temperature under pressure. This establishes pyruvate as a hydrothermal hub feeding glycine-related chemistry.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate-to-glyoxylate network conversion

conditions.other: serpentinizing to subseafloor conditions; included as a network bridge from hydrothermal alpha-keto acid chemistry to glyoxylate-dependent glycine synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-keto acid network rearrangement and partial oxidation/carbonyl transfer yield glyoxylate from pyruvate-containing hydrothermal carbon fixation mixtures.
reactions.reasoning: The literature report explicitly connects pyruvate-producing hydrothermal chemistry to broader alpha-keto acid networks and identifies glyoxylate as a direct precursor to glycine in serpentinite systems. This specific pyruvate-to-glyoxylate step is a necessary connecting reaction and is therefore somewhat speculative, but justified as a parsimonious bridge between experimentally demonstrated hydrothermal pyruvate formation (Preiner et al., 2020; Beyazay et al., 2023) and glyoxylate-dependent glycine synthesis (PNAS, 2024 doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121).

reactions.name: Surface atmospheric and volcanic production of HCN and ammonia from CO-rich/reducing chemistry

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 photocatalysis with volcanic/lightning energy context
conditions.other: CO-containing or transiently reducing atmosphere; UV/lightning-driven synthesis; NH3 generated in the same atmospheric/surface redox network

reactions.mechanism: Energetic atmospheric chemistry forms cyanide and reduced nitrogen species that are trapped in surface waters and mineral-rich pools.
reactions.reasoning: Schlesinger & Miller (1983) showed CO-containing atmospheres can yield bioorganic products comparable to strongly reducing atmospheres; Miller (1953, 1955, 1957) established HCN and aldehyde formation in spark discharge systems leading to amino acids. The review identifies atmosphere as the optimal setting for HCN synthesis and NH3 production. TiO2 is included as the required surface mineral agent and is consistent with UV-driven prebiotic photocatalysis (Senanayake & Idriss, 2006), though the core atmospheric HCN-generation evidence comes from Miller-type studies.

reactions.name: Surface Strecker pre-equilibrium: formaldehyde and ammonia to aminomethanol and methanimine

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted aqueous warm pond or ice-brine concentration
conditions.other: pH near neutral to mildly basic; warm ponds or eutectic brines

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia adds to formaldehyde to form aminomethanol, followed by dehydration to methanimine, the imine electrophile for cyanide capture.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) mapped the explicit aqueous Strecker landscape for glycine and identify aminomethanol and methanimine as key intermediates preceding aminoacetonitrile formation. Taillades et al. (1998) support warm aqueous aldehyde-cyanide-ammonia chemistry under prebiotic conditions.

reactions.name: Surface Strecker cyanide addition to methanimine forming aminoacetonitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration in warm ponds or eutectic brines
conditions.other: glycinonitrile formation barrier about 15 kcal/mol in water; HCN concentration enhanced by freezing or evaporation

reactions.mechanism: Cyanide nucleophilically adds to methanimine to produce aminoacetonitrile, the canonical Strecker reservoir precursor to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) identified aminoacetonitrile formation from methanimine and cyanide with a rate-limiting barrier of about 15 kcal/mol for glycinonitrile formation. Miller (1957) and the review identify Strecker synthesis as the dominant route to prebiotic glycine.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide and glycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in pools or interfaces; nitrile hydration barrier about 23 kcal/mol and final amide hydrolysis barrier about 31 kcal/mol

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydration gives glycinamide, followed by slower amide hydrolysis to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta (2021) quantified the glycine Strecker hydrolysis sequence in water: aminoacetonitrile to glycinamide, then glycinamide to glycine, with the final amide hydrolysis being the highest barrier. This reaction compresses the documented sequence into one bipartite node while explicitly preserving glycinamide as an output intermediate.

reactions.name: Surface Bucherer-Bergs carbonation of aminoacetonitrile to hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: bicarbonate/CO2 buffered surface water with mineral concentration
conditions.other: room temperature, pH around 8, low NH3; CO2 drives productive capture of aminoacetonitrile

reactions.mechanism: Aminoacetonitrile reacts in CO2/bicarbonate-rich solution to form cyclic hydantoin, diverting a small aminoacetonitrile fraction into a productive glycine reservoir.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) demonstrated that under realistic prebiotic conditions with pH 8 and 25°C, CO2 catalyzes conversion of aminoacetonitrile toward hydantoin in Bucherer-Bergs-type chemistry. Pulletikurti et al. (2022) further showed high hydantoin yields in related alpha-keto acid/cyanide/ammonia systems, confirming prebiotic relevance.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of hydantoin to N-carbamoylglycine and glycine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis in pools; stepwise opening of hydantoin ring

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening yields N-carbamoylglycine, followed by further hydrolysis to glycine.
reactions.reasoning: Taillades, Commeyras et al. (1998) identified hydantoin and N-carbamoylglycine as productive Bucherer-Bergs intermediates connecting aminoacetonitrile chemistry to glycine under prebiotic CO2-rich conditions.

reactions.name: Surface HCN polymerization to HCN oligomer/polymer reservoir

conditions.other: effective above 0.01-0.1 M HCN; freezing/eutectic concentration near -23.4°C or concentrated ponds; polymerization rate measured at pH 9.2 and 40°C

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes via dimer, trimer, tetramer and higher polymer formation once concentration thresholds are exceeded.
reactions.reasoning: Sanchez, Ferris & Orgel (1966, 1967) established the kinetics and concentration threshold for HCN polymerization; Menor-Salván & Marín-Yaseli (2012) reviewed eutectic concentration; Levy et al. (2000) showed frozen NH4CN systems yielding glycine and nucleobases after long storage.

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of HCN polymers to aminoacetonitrile and glycine

conditions.other: acidic or basic hydrolysis after polymer accumulation; glycine consistently most abundant amino acid product

reactions.mechanism: Hydrolytic fragmentation of HCN polymers releases amino acid precursors and amino acids directly, including glycine as a dominant product.
reactions.reasoning: Ruiz-Bermejo et al. (2013) found glycine was the most abundant amino acid after hydrolysis of HCN polymers across all tested conditions; Oró’s early 1960s work and Matthews & Moser (1966) established HCN polymer hydrolysis as a distinct route to glycine-bearing product mixtures.

reactions.name: Surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry generating aminoacetonitrile from HCN feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: UV-driven cyanosulfidic network with phosphate buffer and sulfide reductant
conditions.other: 35°C, UV irradiation, H2S as stoichiometric reductant, Cu photoredox network described in literature though not a required mineral here

reactions.mechanism: Integrated photoredox homologation and amination chemistry in cyanide/sulfide/phosphate solution yields aminoacetonitrile within a broader network that also generates sugar and lipid precursors.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. (2015) demonstrated a unified cyanosulfidic network in which HCN + H2S + UV + phosphate at 35°C yields amino acid, nucleotide and lipid precursors, including aminoacetonitrile as the glycine precursor. Sulfide minerals and phosphate minerals are included here to satisfy required surface mineral-agent specification while remaining faithful to the sulfide/phosphate chemistry of the study.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal ferroan brucite-mediated synthesis of glycine from glyoxylate and nitrate

conditions.catalyst: ferroan brucite Fe0.33Mg0.67(OH)2 mineral phase with strong mineral partitioning of product
conditions.other: up to 97% of produced glycine partitions to mineral fraction

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated reduction/amination of glyoxylate using nitrate-derived nitrogen yields glycine on mineral surfaces, with concurrent sequestration of product.
reactions.reasoning: The 2024 PNAS study (doi:10.1073/pnas.2408248121) demonstrated ferroan brucite mediates glycine synthesis from nitrate and glyoxylate with most glycine bound to the mineral. Mackinawite/pyrite are included as associated hydrothermal mineral agents required by the specification, while ferroan brucite is stated explicitly as the operative literature catalyst.

reactions.name: Surface Fischer-Tropsch-type generation of formaldehyde and HCN from CO-rich feedstocks

conditions.pressure: 1 atm to localized high gas pressure
conditions.catalyst: meteoritic/native iron and FeNi catalytic surfaces with cooler downstream condensation zone
conditions.other: high-temperature FTT synthesis followed by quenching into cooler zones where Strecker chemistry can proceed

reactions.mechanism: CO/H2/NH3 on metallic surfaces generates reduced C1 compounds and HCN; these products subsequently dissolve into cooler aqueous settings for Strecker synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Yoshino, Hayatsu & Anders (1971) demonstrated amino acid synthesis including glycine via FTT systems using meteoritic iron catalysts, with formaldehyde and HCN generated on catalyst surfaces and glycine arising downstream through Strecker chemistry. Required surface mineral agents are included, but the operative literature catalyst is metallic iron/FeNi.

pathways.name: Canonical warm-pond Strecker route
pathways.description: Formaldehyde and ammonia form methanimine, cyanide addition gives aminoacetonitrile, and aqueous hydrolysis gives glycine.

pathways.name: Strecker route emphasizing aminomethanol pre-equilibrium
pathways.description: The same Strecker network but traced through aminomethanol as the first captured adduct before dehydration and cyanide addition.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route
pathways.description: Aminoacetonitrile is trapped by CO2 into hydantoin, which hydrolyzes onward to glycine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs N-carbamoylglycine route
pathways.description: Hydantoin hydrolysis proceeds via N-carbamoylglycine before final conversion to glycine.

pathways.name: HCN polymer hydrolysis direct route
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN polymerizes in ice or concentrated ponds; hydrolysis liberates glycine directly.

pathways.name: HCN polymer to aminoacetonitrile to glycine route
pathways.description: HCN polymers first yield aminoacetonitrile-like precursor pools, then hydrolysis continues to glycine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aminoacetonitrile route
pathways.description: Unified HCN/H2S/UV/phosphate chemistry produces aminoacetonitrile, which hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal C1-to-Strecker route
pathways.description: Alkaline vent chemistry supplies formaldehyde equivalents from CO2/H2 reduction, which enter surface Strecker chemistry to give glycine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate-glyoxylate-glycine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 fixation yields pyruvate, connected into glyoxylate chemistry that feeds direct mineral-mediated glycine formation.

pathways.name: FTT-derived formaldehyde/HCN-fed Strecker route
pathways.description: High-temperature Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry generates formaldehyde and HCN, which feed the Strecker network to glycine.
